overview: A comprehensive, beginner-friendly training program for building AI-powered drones, covering four specialized tracks: AI Engineer (Generalist), ML Platform Engineer (MLOps), Data Engineer (Geospatial & Sensor Fusion), and Edge AI Engineer. The curriculum includes deep theoretical knowledge, hands-on implementation, and culminates in a Grand Project: an Autonomous Forest Fire Detection System. A custom hardware setup (based on DJI O4 Air Unit, Pixhawk flight controller, and NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano) is used to apply all concepts in a real-world drone environment.

glossary.definition: Vision Transformer – a neural network architecture that applies the Transformer (originally for NLP) to image classification by treating image patches as tokens.
Convolutional Neural Network – a class of deep neural networks commonly applied to analyzing visual imagery.
Contrastive Language–Image Pre-training – a model that learns visual concepts from natural language descriptions, enabling zero-shot classification and image-text retrieval.
Low-Rank Adaptation – a technique for fine-tuning large language models by adding trainable low-rank matrices to attention layers, significantly reducing memory usage.
Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation – combines quantization (4-bit) with LoRA to further reduce memory footprint during fine-tuning.
Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning – a set of techniques (like LoRA) that adapt large pretrained models with a small number of trainable parameters.
The process of reducing the precision of model weights (e.g., from 32-bit to 8-bit or 4-bit) to reduce memory and accelerate inference.
A technique where a small 'student' model is trained to mimic a larger 'teacher' model, compressing knowledge while retaining performance.
Kullback-Leibler divergence – a measure of how one probability distribution diverges from a second, used as a loss function in knowledge distillation.
Removing neural network weights that are deemed unimportant (e.g., near-zero) to reduce model size and inference time.
Machine Learning Operations – practices for deploying, managing, and monitoring ML models in production.
An open-source container orchestration platform for automating deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications.
Infrastructure as Code tool that allows you to define and provision cloud infrastructure using a declarative configuration language.
A machine learning toolkit for Kubernetes that enables building and deploying scalable ML pipelines.
Data Version Control – a tool for versioning large datasets, models, and experiments, similar to Git but for data.
NVIDIA Triton Inference Server – a platform for serving AI models from multiple frameworks with high performance.
The degradation of model performance over time due to changes in the input data distribution (data drift) or relationships between features and target (concept drift).
A spatial database extender for PostgreSQL, adding support for geographic objects and spatial queries.
SpatioTemporal Asset Catalog – a specification for describing geospatial assets (like satellite or drone imagery) to make them searchable by time and location.
Cloud Optimized GeoTIFF – a GeoTIFF file structure that enables efficient streaming of portions of the image from cloud storage.
Light Detection and Ranging – a remote sensing method that uses laser light to measure distances and create 3D point clouds.
Micro Air Vehicle Link – a lightweight communication protocol for drones and ground stations, used for telemetry and control.
Robot Operating System 2 – a middleware for building robot applications, providing services like hardware abstraction, low-level device control, and message passing.
MAVLink to ROS/ROS2 bridge – a ROS package that allows communication between ROS nodes and autopilots (like Pixhawk) via MAVLink.
NVIDIA's parallel computing platform and programming model for GPUs, enabling general-purpose computing on graphics processing units.
Hardware-in-the-Loop – a testing technique where real hardware (e.g., flight controller) is connected to a simulator that mimics the environment, allowing safe testing.

lessons.title: Architectural Mastery: Vision Transformers & Multi-Modal Models
lessons.detailed_explanation:
Vision Transformers (ViT) have revolutionized computer vision by applying the Transformer architecture originally designed for natural language processing. Instead of using convolutional layers, ViT divides an image into fixed-size patches, linearly embeds them, adds positional information, and then processes them with a standard Transformer encoder. This approach allows the model to capture global relationships across the entire image, often outperforming CNNs on large datasets.

Multi-modal models like CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training) go a step further by jointly training an image encoder and a text encoder so that images and their textual descriptions are mapped to a shared embedding space. This enables powerful applications such as zero-shot classification (classifying images without seeing examples of that class) and natural language image retrieval (e.g., 'show me drone images containing power lines').

For drone applications, using ViT and CLIP can help the AI understand complex scenes from aerial perspectives and respond to natural language queries.

step_by_step_guide.title: Implement a Vision Transformer from scratch
step_by_step_guide.description: Write the core components: patch embedding, multi-head self-attention, and the Transformer encoder.
step_by_step_guide.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class PatchEmbedding(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, img_size=224, patch_size=16, in_channels=3, embed_dim=768):
        super().__init__()
        self.img_size = img_size
        self.patch_size = patch_size
        self.num_patches = (img_size // patch_size) ** 2
        self.proj = nn.Conv2d(in_channels, embed_dim, kernel_size=patch_size, stride=patch_size)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.proj(x)  # (batch, embed_dim, num_patches_h, num_patches_w)
        x = x.flatten(2).transpose(1, 2)  # (batch, num_patches, embed_dim)
        return x

class MultiHeadSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, embed_dim, num_heads):
        super().__init__()
        self.num_heads = num_heads
        self.head_dim = embed_dim // num_heads
        self.scale = self.head_dim ** -0.5
        self.qkv = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim * 3)
        self.proj = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, N, C = x.shape
        qkv = self.qkv(x).reshape(B, N, 3, self.num_heads, self.head_dim).permute(2, 0, 3, 1, 4)
        q, k, v = qkv[0], qkv[1], qkv[2]
        attn = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) * self.scale
        attn = attn.softmax(dim=-1)
        x = (attn @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, N, C)
        x = self.proj(x)
        return x

class TransformerBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, embed_dim, num_heads, mlp_ratio=4.0):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = nn.LayerNorm(embed_dim)
        self.attn = MultiHeadSelfAttention(embed_dim, num_heads)
        self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(embed_dim)
        mlp_hidden = int(embed_dim * mlp_ratio)
        self.mlp = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(embed_dim, mlp_hidden),
            nn.GELU(),
            nn.Linear(mlp_hidden, embed_dim)
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x

class VisionTransformer(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, img_size=224, patch_size=16, in_channels=3, num_classes=1000,
                 embed_dim=768, depth=12, num_heads=12):
        super().__init__()
        self.patch_embed = PatchEmbedding(img_size, patch_size, in_channels, embed_dim)
        self.cls_token = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(1, 1, embed_dim))
        self.pos_embed = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(1, 1 + self.patch_embed.num_patches, embed_dim))
        self.pos_drop = nn.Dropout(0.1)
        self.blocks = nn.ModuleList([
            TransformerBlock(embed_dim, num_heads) for _ in range(depth)
        ])
        self.norm = nn.LayerNorm(embed_dim)
        self.head = nn.Linear(embed_dim, num_classes)

    def forward(self, x):
        B = x.shape[0]
        x = self.patch_embed(x)
        cls_tokens = self.cls_token.expand(B, -1, -1)
        x = torch.cat((cls_tokens, x), dim=1)
        x = x + self.pos_embed
        x = self.pos_drop(x)
        for blk in self.blocks:
            x = blk(x)
        x = self.norm(x)
        cls_out = x[:, 0]
        x = self.head(cls_out)
        return x

# Example usage
model = VisionTransformer(img_size=224, patch_size=16, num_classes=10)
x = torch.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
out = model(x)
print(out.shape)  # Should be [1, 10]

step_by_step_guide.title: Load a pretrained CLIP model
step_by_step_guide.description: Use Hugging Face transformers to load CLIP and test image-text similarity.
step_by_step_guide.code:
from transformers import CLIPProcessor, CLIPModel
import torch
from PIL import Image
import requests

# Load model and processor
model = CLIPModel.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")
processor = CLIPProcessor.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")

# Example: a drone image and text queries
url = "https://example.com/drone_image.jpg"  # Replace with actual image URL
image = Image.open(requests.get(url, stream=True).raw)

texts = ["a photo of a forest", "a photo of a power line", "a photo of a building"]
inputs = processor(text=texts, images=image, return_tensors="pt", padding=True)

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**inputs)
    logits_per_image = outputs.logits_per_image  # image-text similarity
    probs = logits_per_image.softmax(dim=1)

print("Similarity probabilities:", probs)

lessons.title: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)
lessons.detailed_explanation:
Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods allow us to adapt large pre-trained models (like Llama 4 or Mistral) to specific tasks without fine-tuning all parameters. This is crucial when working with consumer GPUs that have limited memory. The most popular technique is LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation). LoRA freezes the pre-trained model weights and injects trainable rank decomposition matrices into the layers of the Transformer architecture. This drastically reduces the number of trainable parameters (sometimes by >99%) while achieving comparable performance to full fine-tuning. QLoRA (Quantized LoRA) goes further by quantizing the base model to 4-bit, enabling fine-tuning of even larger models on a single GPU.

For drone applications, PEFT allows you to adapt a large vision-language model to understand specific drone-related commands (like 'hover near the power line' or 'avoid trees') without needing massive compute resources.

step_by_step_guide.title: Install required libraries
step_by_step_guide.description: Install Hugging Face transformers, peft, bitsandbytes (for QLoRA), and datasets.
step_by_step_guide.code: pip install transformers peft bitsandbytes datasets accelerate

step_by_step_guide.title: Load a pre-trained model (e.g., Mistral) with 4-bit quantization (QLoRA)
step_by_step_guide.description: Use bitsandbytes to load the model in 4-bit.
step_by_step_guide.code:
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, BitsAndBytesConfig
import torch

model_name = "mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1"

bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.float16,
)

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_name,
    quantization_config=bnb_config,
    device_map="auto",
    trust_remote_code=True
)
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token

step_by_step_guide.title: Apply LoRA adapters
step_by_step_guide.description: Use PEFT to add LoRA layers to the model.
step_by_step_guide.code:
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model

lora_config = LoraConfig(
    r=8,                     # rank
    lora_alpha=32,           # scaling factor
    target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj"],  # which modules to apply LoRA to
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    bias="none",
    task_type="CAUSAL_LM"
)

model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()  # Should show very few trainable parameters

step_by_step_guide.title: Prepare a dataset for fine-tuning (e.g., drone instruction dataset)
step_by_step_guide.description: Create a dataset of prompts and responses relevant to drone commands.

step_by_step_guide.title: Set up training arguments and fine-tune
step_by_step_guide.description: Use the Trainer API to fine-tune the LoRA adapters.
step_by_step_guide.code:
from transformers import TrainingArguments, Trainer

training_args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="./drone-lora",
    per_device_train_batch_size=4,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=4,
    num_train_epochs=3,
    logging_steps=10,
    save_strategy="epoch",
    learning_rate=2e-4,
    fp16=True,
    optim="paged_adamw_8bit",
)

trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    args=training_args,
    train_dataset=dataset,
    tokenizer=tokenizer,
    data_collator=lambda data: tokenizer([d["text"] for d in data], truncation=True, padding=True, return_tensors="pt"),
)

trainer.train()

step_by_step_guide.title: Save and test the fine-tuned model
step_by_step_guide.description: Save the LoRA adapters and use the model for inference.
step_by_step_guide.code:
# Save the adapter weights
model.save_pretrained("./drone-lora-adapter")
tokenizer.save_pretrained("./drone-lora-adapter")

# Load the base model again and apply the adapter for inference
from peft import PeftModel
base_model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_name, device_map="auto")
model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base_model, "./drone-lora-adapter")

# Test
prompt = "Instruction: Avoid the tree\nResponse:"
inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").to("cuda")
outputs = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=50)
print(tokenizer.decode(outputs[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

lessons.title: Production Optimization Stack: Quantization & Distillation
lessons.detailed_explanation: Once a model is trained, deploying it to edge devices like the Jetson Orin Nano requires optimization for size and speed. Two key techniques are quantization and knowledge distillation. Quantization reduces the precision of model weights (e.g., from 32-bit floating point to 8-bit or 4-bit integer), which cuts memory usage and speeds up inference. NVIDIA's Model Optimizer (part of TensorRT) automates this with minimal accuracy loss. Knowledge distillation involves training a smaller 'student' model to mimic a larger 'teacher' model. The student learns to match the teacher's soft outputs (using KL divergence loss), resulting in a compact model that retains much of the teacher's performance. For drones, distillation can produce a lightweight model suitable for real-time obstacle detection.

step_by_step_guide.title: Install NVIDIA TensorRT and Model Optimizer
step_by_step_guide.description: On your Jetson or development machine, install TensorRT and the necessary Python bindings.
step_by_step_guide.code:
# On Jetson: JetPack includes TensorRT. 
# On PC: Follow NVIDIA's installation guide: https://developer.nvidia.com/tensorrt
pip install tensorrt torch2trt

step_by_step_guide.title: Quantize a PyTorch model using NVIDIA Model Optimizer
step_by_step_guide.description: Take a pretrained model and apply INT8 quantization.
step_by_step_guide.code:
import torch
import torchvision.models as models
from torch2trt import torch2trt

# Load a pretrained model (e.g., ResNet)
model = models.resnet18(pretrained=True).cuda().eval()

# Create dummy input
x = torch.randn(1, 3, 224, 224).cuda()

# Convert to TensorRT with INT8 precision (requires calibration data)
model_trt = torch2trt(model, [x], fp16_mode=False, int8_mode=True, int8_calib_dataset=calib_data)

# Save the optimized model
torch.save(model_trt.state_dict(), 'model_trt_int8.pth')

step_by_step_guide.title: Implement Knowledge Distillation
step_by_step_guide.description: Train a small student model (e.g., MobileNetV4) to mimic a larger teacher (e.g., ViT).
step_by_step_guide.code:
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

# Assume teacher_model and student_model are defined and loaded
# Teacher is large, student is small

class DistillationLoss(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, temperature=4.0, alpha=0.7):
        super().__init__()
        self.temperature = temperature
        self.alpha = alpha
        self.ce_loss = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

    def forward(self, student_logits, teacher_logits, labels):
        # Soft loss: KL divergence between softened outputs
        soft_loss = F.kl_div(
            F.log_softmax(student_logits / self.temperature, dim=1),
            F.softmax(teacher_logits / self.temperature, dim=1),
            reduction='batchmean'
        ) * (self.temperature ** 2)
        # Hard loss: standard cross-entropy with true labels
        hard_loss = self.ce_loss(student_logits, labels)
        # Combined loss
        return self.alpha * soft_loss + (1 - self.alpha) * hard_loss

# Training loop example
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(student_model.parameters(), lr=1e-4)
for images, labels in dataloader:
    images, labels = images.cuda(), labels.cuda()
    with torch.no_grad():
        teacher_logits = teacher_model(images)
    student_logits = student_model(images)
    loss = distillation_loss(student_logits, teacher_logits, labels)
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()

lessons.title: Sparsity & Pruning
lessons.detailed_explanation: Pruning removes weights from a neural network that have near-zero importance, reducing model size and inference time. Unstructured pruning zeros out individual weights, leading to sparse weight matrices. This can achieve up to 40% size reduction with less than 1% accuracy loss when combined with fine-tuning. After pruning, the model can be stored in sparse format and accelerated using specialized libraries (e.g., cuSPARSE). For drone AI, pruning can make the model fit within tight memory constraints without sacrificing performance.

step_by_step_guide.title: Train a baseline model
step_by_step_guide.description: Start with a pretrained model or train one on your dataset.
step_by_step_guide.code:
# Assume we have a trained model in PyTorch
model = torch.load('baseline_model.pth')
model.eval()

step_by_step_guide.title: Apply unstructured pruning
step_by_step_guide.description: Use PyTorch's pruning utilities to zero out weights with low magnitude.

step_by_step_guide.title: Fine-tune the pruned model
step_by_step_guide.description: Re-train the model briefly to recover accuracy.
step_by_step_guide.code:
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-4)
for epoch in range(5):  # Few epochs of fine-tuning
    for images, labels in train_loader:
        images, labels = images.cuda(), labels.cuda()
        outputs = model(images)
        loss = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()(outputs, labels)
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()

step_by_step_guide.title: Save the sparse model
step_by_step_guide.description: Save the model with sparsity.
step_by_step_guide.code: torch.save(model.state_dict(), 'pruned_model.pth')

step_by_step_guide.title: Measure size reduction and accuracy
step_by_step_guide.description: Compute the sparsity level and check accuracy.
step_by_step_guide.code:
def compute_sparsity(model):
    total_zeros = 0
    total_params = 0
    for param in model.parameters():
        total_zeros += (param == 0).sum().item()
        total_params += param.numel()
    return 100.0 * total_zeros / total_params

print(f"Sparsity: {compute_sparsity(model):.2f}%")

# Evaluate accuracy
correct = 0
total = 0
with torch.no_grad():
    for images, labels in test_loader:
        images, labels = images.cuda(), labels.cuda()
        outputs = model(images)
        _, predicted = torch.max(outputs, 1)
        total += labels.size(0)
        correct += (predicted == labels).sum().item()
print(f"Accuracy: {100 * correct / total:.2f}%")

lessons.title: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) for AI
lessons.detailed_explanation: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) allows you to define and manage your cloud infrastructure using code instead of manual processes. For AI workloads, this means you can script the creation of GPU-enabled Kubernetes clusters, storage buckets, and networking. Terraform (or Pulumi) is used to declare the desired state of infrastructure. The NVIDIA GPU Operator simplifies managing GPUs on Kubernetes, automatically installing drivers and exposing GPU resources to pods. Karpenter dynamically scales your cluster based on pending workloads, ensuring you only pay for what you use. This is essential for cost-efficient training and serving at scale.

step_by_step_guide.title: Install Terraform and configure AWS credentials
step_by_step_guide.description: Download Terraform and set up AWS access keys.
step_by_step_guide.code:

unzip terraform_1.5.0_linux_amd64.zip
sudo mv terraform /usr/local/bin/

# Configure AWS credentials (or set environment variables)
aws configure

step_by_step_guide.description: Run terraform init and apply to create the infrastructure.
step_by_step_guide.code:

step_by_step_guide.code:

    --namespace gpu-operator --create-namespace

step_by_step_guide.description: Deploy Karpenter to automatically add nodes based on pending pods.
step_by_step_guide.code:
helm repo add karpenter https://charts.karpenter.sh
helm repo update
helm install karpenter karpenter/karpenter \
    --namespace karpenter --create-namespace \
    --set serviceAccount.annotations.eks\.amazonaws\.com/role-arn=arn:aws:iam::${ACCOUNT_ID}:role/KarpenterControllerRole \
    --set settings.clusterName=gpu-cluster \
    --set settings.interruptionQueue=${QUEUE_NAME}

step_by_step_guide.description: Run a test pod to ensure GPUs are exposed.
step_by_step_guide.code: kubectl run gpu-test --rm -it --restart=Never --image=nvidia/cuda:11.0-base --limits=nvidia.com/gpu=1 -- nvidia-smi

lessons.title: Workflow Orchestration & Data Versioning
lessons.detailed_explanation: ML pipelines require repeatable and modular workflows. Kubeflow Pipelines allows you to define each step (data preparation, training, evaluation) as a containerized component. You can then run these pipelines on Kubernetes, track artifacts, and reuse components across projects. Data Version Control (DVC) complements this by versioning large datasets (500GB+) in a Git-like manner. It stores data in cloud storage and keeps pointers in Git, ensuring that every model artifact is linked to the exact data version used. This is crucial for reproducibility and debugging.

step_by_step_guide.title: Install Kubeflow Pipelines on your cluster
step_by_step_guide.description: Deploy Kubeflow Pipelines using Kustomize.
step_by_step_guide.code:
kubectl create namespace kubeflow
kubectl apply -k "github.com/kubeflow/pipelines/manifests/kustomize/cluster-scoped-resources?ref=2.0.0"
kubectl wait --for condition=established --timeout=60s crd/applications.app.k8s.io
kubectl apply -k "github.com/kubeflow/pipelines/manifests/kustomize/env/platform-agnostic?ref=2.0.0"

step_by_step_guide.title: Define a Kubeflow pipeline component
step_by_step_guide.description: Write a Python function that defines a component.

step_by_step_guide.title: Upload and run the pipeline
step_by_step_guide.description: Use the Kubeflow Pipelines UI or Python client to upload and execute.
step_by_step_guide.code:
import kfp
client = kfp.Client()
run = client.create_run_from_pipeline_func(pipeline, arguments={'data_path': 's3://my-bucket/data.csv'})
print(f"Run ID: {run.run_id}")

step_by_step_guide.title: Install and initialize DVC
step_by_step_guide.description: Set up DVC for data versioning.
step_by_step_guide.code:
pip install dvc
# In your Git repository
git init
dvc init
dvc remote add -d myremote s3://my-bucket/dvc-store
# Add dataset
dvc add data/drone_images
git add data/drone_images.dvc .gitignore
git commit -m "Add dataset"
# Push data to remote
dvc push

step_by_step_guide.title: Link model artifacts to data version
step_by_step_guide.description: Use DVC to track model artifacts and ensure lineage.
step_by_step_guide.code:
# After training, add model artifact
dvc add models/model.pth
git add models/model.pth.dvc
git commit -m "Add trained model"
# Tag the data version
git tag -a v1.0 -m "Dataset version 1"
# Push data and model
dvc push

lessons.title: Model Serving & Observability
lessons.detailed_explanation: NVIDIA Triton Inference Server is a high-performance serving engine that supports multiple frameworks (PyTorch, ONNX, TensorRT, etc.) and features like dynamic batching and concurrency. It can be deployed on Kubernetes for scalability. Observability is crucial to ensure that deployed models remain reliable. Prometheus collects metrics (e.g., inference latency, GPU utilization) and Grafana visualizes them. Evidently AI monitors data and model drift by comparing incoming data distributions against reference data. This allows you to detect when real-world data diverges from training data and take corrective actions like retraining.

step_by_step_guide.description: Organize your model files according to Triton's expected structure.

# Copy model file (e.g., model.pt) and create a config.pbtxt
# Example config.pbtxt:
# name: "resnet"
# platform: "pytorch_libtorch"
# max_batch_size: 8
# input [
#   { name: "input" data_type: TYPE_FP32 dims: [3, 224, 224] }

# output [
#   { name: "output" data_type: TYPE_FP32 dims: [1000] }
# ]

step_by_step_guide.code:

  - job_name: 'triton'

      - targets: ['triton-service:8002']

step_by_step_guide.description: Run a Python service to analyze incoming data vs reference.
step_by_step_guide.code:
import evidently
from evidently.report import Report
from evidently.metric_preset import DataDriftPreset

# Load reference data (training data) and current data (production data)
reference_data = pd.read_csv('training_data.csv')
current_data = pd.read_csv('today_inference_inputs.csv')

data_drift_report = Report(metrics=[DataDriftPreset()])
data_drift_report.run(reference_data=reference_data, current_data=current_data)
data_drift_report.save_html('drift_report.html')

lessons.title: Geospatial Intelligence (GeoAI)
lessons.detailed_explanation: Geospatial intelligence involves managing and querying spatial data. PostGIS is a spatial database extension for PostgreSQL that adds support for geographic objects and spatial queries. For example, you can write SQL to find all images taken within a certain distance of a point or polygon. STAC (SpatioTemporal Asset Catalog) is a specification for making geospatial assets searchable by time and location. A STAC API allows you to catalog drone images and retrieve them based on coordinates and time ranges. Cloud Optimized GeoTIFFs (COGs) are a special file format that enables efficient streaming of large geospatial images from cloud storage; you can load only the pixels you need without downloading the whole file.

step_by_step_guide.title: Install PostGIS and create a spatial database
step_by_step_guide.description: Set up PostgreSQL with PostGIS extension.
step_by_step_guide.code:
# On Ubuntu
sudo apt install postgresql postgis
sudo -u postgres psql -c "CREATE DATABASE drone_geo;"
sudo -u postgres psql -d drone_geo -c "CREATE EXTENSION postgis;"

step_by_step_guide.description: Create a table with geometry column and insert data.

with rasterio.open('output_cog.tif') as src:
    # Read a 1000x1000 pixel window at location (x=0, y=0)
    window = rasterio.windows.Window(0, 0, 1000, 1000)
    data = src.read(1, window=window)

lessons.title: Sensor Fusion Pipelines
lessons.detailed_explanation: Sensor fusion combines data from multiple sensors (e.g., camera, LiDAR, GPS, IMU) to create a more complete understanding of the environment. LiDAR processing involves cleaning point clouds (removing noise) and registering them to create 3D maps. Telemetry from the flight controller (MAVLink messages) arrives at high frequency (100Hz) while camera triggers are slower (2Hz). To tag each image with accurate position, you interpolate between telemetry points. This ensures that every image has a precise geolocation, enabling accurate mapping and analysis.

step_by_step_guide.title: Process LiDAR point cloud with Open3D
step_by_step_guide.description: Load a point cloud, apply statistical outlier removal, and visualize.
step_by_step_guide.code:
import open3d as o3d

# Load point cloud
pcd = o3d.io.read_point_cloud("lidar_scan.pcd")

# Remove statistical outliers
cl, ind = pcd.remove_statistical_outlier(nb_neighbors=20, std_ratio=2.0)
filtered_pcd = pcd.select_by_index(ind)

# Visualize
o3d.visualization.draw_geometries([filtered_pcd])

# Save filtered
o3d.io.write_point_cloud("filtered_scan.pcd", filtered_pcd)

step_by_step_guide.title: Perform point-to-plane registration (ICP)
step_by_step_guide.description: Align two point clouds using ICP with point-to-plane metric.
step_by_step_guide.code:
# Assume source and target point clouds
source = o3d.io.read_point_cloud("scan1.pcd")
target = o3d.io.read_point_cloud("scan2.pcd")

# Downsample for speed
source_down = source.voxel_down_sample(0.05)
target_down = target.voxel_down_sample(0.05)

# Compute normals for target
source_down.estimate_normals(o3d.geometry.KDTreeSearchParamHybrid(radius=0.1, max_nn=30))
target_down.estimate_normals(o3d.geometry.KDTreeSearchParamHybrid(radius=0.1, max_nn=30))

# ICP with point-to-plane
reg_p2p = o3d.pipelines.registration.registration_icp(
    source_down, target_down, max_correspondence_distance=0.2,
    estimation_method=o3d.pipelines.registration.TransformationEstimationPointToPlane()
)
print(reg_p2p.transformation)

# Apply transformation to full source
source.transform(reg_p2p.transformation)
o3d.io.write_point_cloud("aligned_scan.pcd", source)

step_by_step_guide.title: Sync MAVLink telemetry with camera triggers
step_by_step_guide.description: Subscribe to MAVLink messages and interpolate to assign coordinates to each image.
step_by_step_guide.code:
from pymavlink import mavutil
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Connect to telemetry (serial or UDP)
master = mavutil.mavlink_connection('/dev/ttyACM0', baud=57600)

# Collect telemetry and camera timestamps
telemetry_data = []  # list of (timestamp, lat, lon, alt)
camera_timestamps = []  # list of timestamps when images were taken

# In a loop
while True:
    msg = master.recv_match()
    if msg is not None:
        if msg.get_type() == 'GPS_RAW_INT':
            t = time.time()
            lat = msg.lat / 1e7
            lon = msg.lon / 1e7
            alt = msg.alt / 1e3
            telemetry_data.append((t, lat, lon, alt))
        elif msg.get_type() == 'CAMERA_TRIGGER':
            camera_timestamps.append(time.time())

# After flight, create DataFrames and interpolate
telemetry_df = pd.DataFrame(telemetry_data, columns=['timestamp', 'lat', 'lon', 'alt'])
camera_timestamps = np.array(camera_timestamps)

# Interpolate for each camera timestamp
interpolated = []
for cam_ts in camera_timestamps:
    idx = np.searchsorted(telemetry_df['timestamp'].values, cam_ts)
    if idx == 0 or idx == len(telemetry_df):
        continue
    t1, lat1, lon1, alt1 = telemetry_df.iloc[idx-1]
    t2, lat2, lon2, alt2 = telemetry_df.iloc[idx]
    frac = (cam_ts - t1) / (t2 - t1)
    lat = lat1 + frac * (lat2 - lat1)
    lon = lon1 + frac * (lon2 - lon1)
    alt = alt1 + frac * (alt2 - alt1)
    interpolated.append((cam_ts, lat, lon, alt))

# Save geotagged images
for i, (ts, lat, lon, alt) in enumerate(interpolated):
    print(f"Image {i}: lat={lat}, lon={lon}, alt={alt}")
    # Write to EXIF or CSV

lessons.title: Labeling & QA with Active Learning
lessons.detailed_explanation: Active learning is a strategy to improve model efficiency by selecting the most informative data points for human labeling. Instead of labeling random data, you use your model to predict on unlabeled data and select samples where the model is least confident. These 'hard examples' are then sent to human annotators (using tools like CVAT or Labelbox). After labeling, you retrain the model, and the cycle repeats. This reduces labeling costs and focuses effort on data that will most improve the model.

step_by_step_guide.title: Run inference on unlabeled data and compute confidence
step_by_step_guide.description: Use your model to predict on a set of unlabeled images and record the confidence scores.
step_by_step_guide.code:
import torch
from torchvision import transforms
from PIL import Image

model.eval()
unlabeled_images = [...]  # list of image paths
confidences = []

for img_path in unlabeled_images:
    img = Image.open(img_path).convert('RGB')
    img_tensor = transform(img).unsqueeze(0).cuda()
    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model(img_tensor)
        probs = torch.softmax(outputs, dim=1)
        confidence, pred = torch.max(probs, dim=1)
        confidences.append((img_path, confidence.item(), pred.item()))

# Sort by confidence (lowest first)
confidences.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])

step_by_step_guide.title: Select low-confidence samples for labeling
step_by_step_guide.description: Take the top N samples with lowest confidence.
step_by_step_guide.code: low_confidence_samples = confidences[:100]  # top 100 lowest confidence

step_by_step_guide.description: Create a task in CVAT with those images.
step_by_step_guide.code:
# Using CVAT CLI
cvat-cli --auth user:pass task create --name "ActiveLearningBatch" --labels label1 label2 --bug-tracker ''
cvat-cli --auth user:pass task add-images <task_id> path/to/images/*
# Alternatively, use Labelbox API

step_by_step_guide.description: Download labeled data and retrain the model.
step_by_step_guide.code:
# Download annotations from CVAT (e.g., COCO format)
# Then add to training dataset and retrain
model.train()
for epoch in range(5):
    for images, labels in new_annotations_loader:
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        outputs = model(images)
        loss = loss_fn(outputs, labels)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()

step_by_step_guide.title: Repeat the active learning loop
step_by_step_guide.description: Continue to improve model by selecting new low-confidence samples each cycle.
step_by_step_guide.code:
while not convergence:
    # 1. Predict on unlabeled pool
    # 2. Select low-confidence samples
    # 3. Send to labeling
    # 4. Retrain model
    # 5. Evaluate

lessons.title: Robotics Middleware: ROS 2 & MAVROS
lessons.detailed_explanation: ROS 2 (Robot Operating System) provides a flexible framework for building robot software with nodes that communicate via topics, services, and actions. For drones, MAVROS is a ROS 2 package that bridges MAVLink messages to ROS 2 topics. This allows you to control the drone (e.g., send velocity commands) and receive telemetry from the Pixhawk flight controller. Micro-ROS enables running ROS 2 on microcontrollers (like STM32) for low-level control. This stack forms the backbone of autonomous drone software.

step_by_step_guide.title: Create a ROS 2 node for obstacle avoidance
step_by_step_guide.description: Write a node that subscribes to camera topics and publishes velocity commands.
step_by_step_guide.code:
# obstacle_avoidance_node.py
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from sensor_msgs.msg import Image
from geometry_msgs.msg import Twist

class ObstacleAvoidanceNode(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('obstacle_avoidance')
        self.subscription = self.create_subscription(Image, '/camera/image', self.image_callback, 10)
        self.publisher = self.create_publisher(Twist, '/mavros/setpoint_velocity/cmd_vel_unstamped', 10)
        self.get_logger().info('Node started')

    def image_callback(self, msg):
        # Process image, detect obstacles
        # If obstacle, publish stop command
        twist = Twist()
        twist.linear.x = 0.0
        twist.linear.y = 0.0
        twist.linear.z = 0.0
        self.publisher.publish(twist)

def main(args=None):
    rclpy.init(args=args)
    node = ObstacleAvoidanceNode()
    rclpy.spin(node)
    node.destroy_node()
    rclpy.shutdown()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

lessons.title: Optimization for Survival: HIL & Latency Profiling
lessons.detailed_explanation: Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) testing allows you to simulate the drone's flight while using the actual flight controller and AI computer. NVIDIA Isaac Sim provides a photorealistic simulation environment with ROS 2 integration. You can feed simulated camera data to the real Jetson and test the AI software safely. Latency profiling with Nsight Systems helps you identify bottlenecks: is it the image capture, model inference, or command transmission? By measuring each stage, you can optimize the critical path.

grand_project.name: Autonomous Forest Fire Detection System
grand_project.description: A fully integrated system combining all four tracks: the drone autonomously patrols a forest area, uses thermal and vision AI to detect smoke, avoids obstacles, and reports fire locations. The system includes a cloud back-end for data logging, model retraining, and fleet management.

components.contribution: Processes thermal and LiDAR data to create a 3D map of the forest. Uses PostGIS to store geotagged images and STAC API to catalog them. Implements COGs for efficient streaming of aerial maps.
Trains a Vision Transformer (ViT) optimized for small smoke plume detection using knowledge distillation to produce a lightweight student model. Quantizes the model to INT8 for deployment on Jetson.
Deploys the quantized model on Jetson Orin Nano with DeepStream pipeline. Integrates with ROS 2 and MAVROS for real-time obstacle avoidance and flight control. Uses CUDA for image preprocessing.

Implements an OTA update pipeline using Kubernetes and Kubeflow. Automatically uploads 'near miss' obstacles to the cloud, triggers active learning, and retrains the model. Manages model versions with DVC and serves new models via Triton.

hardware_setup.description: A custom-built 7-inch long-range drone with a high-performance AI computer, designed to carry the Jetson Orin Nano and support all training tracks.

components.name: DJI O4 Air Unit Pro
components.role: Provides HD video feed to the pilot (via Goggles N3) and control signals to the flight controller. Also offers telemetry data (GPS, altitude) that can be used for geotagging.

components.name: Pixhawk 6C / Matek H743-WLN Flight Controller
components.role: Runs ArduPilot or PX4 firmware; handles flight stabilization and communicates with the AI computer via MAVLink.

components.name: NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano (8GB)
components.role: The AI brain. Runs deep learning models, processes camera feeds, and sends commands to the flight controller. Also hosts the MLOps stack for over-the-air updates.

components.name: Luxonis OAK-D Lite Camera
components.role: Provides a stereo vision feed for the AI (since the DJI O4 feed is proprietary and cannot be directly accessed by the Jetson). Used for obstacle detection and scene understanding.

components.name: 7-inch Long Range Frame (e.g., iFlight Chimera7)
components.role: Carbon fiber frame large enough to hold the Jetson, flight controller, and a 6S 5000mAh battery.

components.name: DJI Remote 3 & Goggles N3
components.role: Pilot control and video display. Provides a safety override: in case of AI failure, the pilot can flick a switch to regain manual control.

utilization_by_track.AI Engineer: Uses the OAK-D camera to capture training data; tests models in real flights.
utilization_by_track.ML Platform Engineer: Deploys Docker containers to the Jetson; performs OTA updates.
utilization_by_track.Data Engineer: Tags every image from the OAK-D with GPS/telemetry from the O4 Air Unit to build geospatial datasets.
utilization_by_track.Edge AI Engineer: Implements real-time obstacle avoidance by sending commands from the Jetson to the Pixhawk.

hardware_setup.safety_note: Before any real flight, practice in a simulator (Liftoff or DJI Virtual Flight) using the Remote 3 to master manual mode. Always keep the pilot in control with a safety switch that overrides AI commands.

week1_setup_guide.title: Week 1: Purchase & Initial Setup
steps: Order all hardware components listed above. Ensure you have a soldering iron, LiPo battery charger, and basic drone assembly tools.
Assemble the drone frame, mount the Pixhawk and DJI O4 Air Unit. Follow a standard 7-inch build tutorial (e.g., Joshua Bardwell on YouTube).
Install ArduPilot or PX4 on the Pixhawk and configure it with Mission Planner or QGroundControl.
Set up the NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano with JetPack SDK (flash SD card, install CUDA, DeepStream).
Connect the OAK-D camera to the Jetson and run the depthai demo to verify functionality.
Install ROS 2 Humble on the Jetson and establish MAVROS connection to the Pixhawk via UART or USB.
Test basic telemetry by running `ros2 topic echo /mavros/state`.
